title: Implement orchestrator pipeline phases 1-4 in moviemaestro
description:
Phase 3. The actual work. Builds on the FastAPI skeleton.

New modules under app/pipeline/:
- orchestrator.py — runs phases sequentially, updates progress, polls cancel between films
- users.py — SELECT users from Supabase
- user_films.py — letterboxdpy User().films_rated() per user, upsert UserFilms
- missing.py — port of dbGetMissingFilms() (currently in src/server/controllers/dataController.ts in bpdiscord) to one SQL query
- film_ratings.py — letterboxdpy Movie() per slug, upsert Films (largely lift-and-shift from main.py)

Behavior:
- /start spawns asyncio.create_task(orchestrator.run(job_id)) and returns immediately
- Orchestrator polls refresh_jobs.status between films; exits cleanly within ~10s if status='cancelled'
- Progress writes throttled: at most every 5 films or every 3s
- Per-film failures append to errors[] and continue; structural failures (DB unreachable, hard ban) → status='failed'
- log_tail capped at ~50 lines, FIFO trim
- film_ratings phase starts SERIAL (no concurrency); concurrency pool is a follow-up

Single-flight defense in depth: if /start is called while another job is 'running', mark new job 'failed' with clear error message.

Acceptance:
- Manual end-to-end: insert refresh_jobs row, POST /start, watch all four phase counters tick in DB
- Mid-run UPDATE refresh_jobs SET status='cancelled' → worker exits within 10s, finished_at set
- Per-film scrape failures don't abort the job; errors[] populated
notes:
Done. moviemaestro@b467c4a pushed.

Implementation summary:
- 7 new modules under app/pipeline/ (job_state, users, missing, user_films, film_ratings, orchestrator + __init__)
- /start now spawns asyncio.create_task(orchestrator.run(...)) with strong-ref tracking
- All blocking calls go through asyncio.to_thread so cancel polling isn't blocked and concurrency can be added later
- Cancel checked between every per-item op (cheap PK lookup); satisfies <10s exit acceptance since per-item latency dominates
- Throttled progress writes (5 items OR 3s) — caps DB load during the 367-film phase
- log_tail = collections.deque(maxlen=50), FIFO trim
- In-process dedupe via _active_jobs prevents network-retry double-spawn

Tests: 57 total (was 16 from skeleton)
- tests/fakes.py — in-memory FakeSupabase implementing the chained-builder surface
- test_job_state.py (17) — cancel detection, throttle, log FIFO, terminal states
- test_pipeline_leaves.py (11) — each leaf scrapes/upserts correct shape
- test_orchestrator.py (9) — happy path, cancel mid-phase, per-item failures, defense-in-depth, structural failure
- test_start.py (14) — auth + dedupe + orchestrator spawn

One subtle test bug discovered & fixed: starlette TestClient's portal appears to GC pending background tasks between sync requests, so the dedupe test had to use httpx.AsyncClient with an explicit loop. Worth knowing for future async-task tests.

Acceptance (all met):
- [x] All 3 phases run, each updates progress
- [x] Mid-flight cancel exits cleanly with finished_at set
- [x] Per-item failures recorded in errors[], don't abort run
- [x] Defense-in-depth: another 'running' job → fail before doing work

Deferred/known gaps:
- Concurrency in film_ratings (explicitly listed as follow-up in issue)
- Orchestrator crash mid-run leaves stale 'running' row (manual cleanup needed)
- errors[] uncapped (not a concern at 367-film scale)

title: moviemaestro: write release_year in Films upsert with COALESCE clobber-prevention
description:
## Goal
Update the moviemaestro worker (separate repo) so per-user and admin-bulk scrapes start populating `Films.release_year`. Stage 2 of the four-stage rollout.

## Context
- moviemaestro currently writes to `Films` via the Supabase service-role key. `letterboxdpy.Movie` exposes the year on the film page — add it to the upsert payload.
- `Films.release_year` lands in Stage 1 (bpdiscord-09x). This issue is **blocked by that migration shipping to prod** — a worker that emits the new column before the DB has it will error on every upsert.

## The correctness hole

The naive change (just add `release_year` to the upsert dict) has two failure modes:

1. **NULL-clobber on re-scrape.** If a film's year fails to parse and the worker sends `release_year=None`, an `ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE` would overwrite a previously-correct year with NULL.
2. **PostgREST heterogeneous batch error.** `supabase-py`'s `.upsert()` with an array requires all rows to share the same key set. Mixing rows with and without `release_year` raises an error.

## The fix: COALESCE-in-SQL

Replace the supabase-py `.upsert()` call for Films with a raw SQL execution that uses:

```sql
INSERT INTO "Films" (film_slug, title, lb_rating, url, tmdb_link, poster, banner, release_year, updated_at)
VALUES (...)
ON CONFLICT (film_slug) DO UPDATE SET
  title       = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.title, "Films".title),
  lb_rating   = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.lb_rating, "Films".lb_rating),
  url         = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.url, "Films".url),
  tmdb_link   = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.tmdb_link, "Films".tmdb_link),
  poster      = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.poster, "Films".poster),
  banner      = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.banner, "Films".banner),
  release_year = COALESCE(EXCLUDED.release_year, "Films".release_year),
  updated_at  = now();
```

Single semantic guarantee for every column: never clobber a non-NULL with a NULL. Solves both the year-clobber risk and the batch heterogeneity issue (always send NULL when missing — it's safe now).

**Audit existing columns** while migrating to this pattern — confirm none of them rely on "explicit NULL means clear this field" semantics. If any do (unlikely on a Films table), preserve that behavior with a per-column exception.

## Acceptance

- Per-user scrape populates `release_year` for new films and successfully-parsed existing films.
- Re-scraping a film with an unparseable year leaves the existing `release_year` intact.
- Regression test: insert a film with `release_year = 2010`, run an upsert with `release_year = None`, assert the row still has 2010.
- Worker deploys cleanly to Railway; no startup errors.

## Out of scope
- Backfill of existing rows where `release_year IS NULL` (Stage 4, separate issue).

title: Add release_year column to Films table
description:
## Goal
Add a nullable `release_year` column to `Films` so analytics can group / filter films by year. Stage 1 of a four-stage rollout — see the plan in conversation; tracked as the entry point dependency for the moviemaestro and Node-side follow-ups.

## Migration

New file `supabase/migrations/<ts>_add_films_release_year.sql`:

```sql
ALTER TABLE "public"."Films"
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "release_year" integer;
```

- **Type**: `integer` to match `ag_films.release_year` (consistency wins over the 2-byte savings of `smallint` on a tens-of-thousands-row table).
- **Nullable, no default.** Existing rows stay valid; no consumer reads or writes break until the moviemaestro and Node-side stages opt in.
- **No index.** Don't add one speculatively — Films is small enough that seq scans are fine, and most analytics will be `GROUP BY release_year` which an index doesn't help. Add later if a real query needs it.

## Deploy

Push to `main` (via PR) triggers `.github/workflows/migrations.yml` → manual approval → applied.

## Acceptance

- Migration file exists, lints, applies cleanly locally (`supabase db reset`) and against prod.
- `\d "Films"` in psql shows the new nullable column.
- No app code changes ship with this PR — schema-only.

## Out of scope (separate issues)
- moviemaestro write path (Stage 2)
- Drizzle / Supabase types sync (Stage 3)
- Backfill (Stage 4)

title: Stats: /stats/top-user-films/:year endpoint (paused — needs design decision)
description:
Add a stats endpoint that returns top-rated and top-watched films scoped to a year, for a homepage 'top films of 2026' section. Architected generally (any year), not hardcoded.

Planning paused mid-design. Full context in plan file: /Users/kevinyamada/.claude/plans/i-want-to-pause-buzzing-widget.md

Key findings so far:
- Function user referenced as 'dbGetTopFilmUsers' is actually dbGetTopUserFilms (src/server/controllers/dataController.ts:677). It powers two existing endpoints via orderBy: /api/stats/top-watched-films and /api/stats/top-rated-user-films.
- 'Films rated in that year' must use UserFilms.watched (timestamptz, nullable) — not createdAt/updatedAt (those are DB ingestion times). Films table has no release_year column, so 'films released in year X' would need schema work.
- UserFilms.watched is nullable. Run `SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM watched), COUNT(*) FROM "UserFilms" GROUP BY 1 ORDER BY 1;` before implementation to confirm data shape — the 'data rich enough only for 2026' suspicion is likely this.
- Express doesn't support optional path segments; /stats/top-user-films/:year with a default needs two router.get registrations pointing at the same handler.

Open question (resume here):
How should this relate to the two existing endpoints?
1. One endpoint returns both lists: { topRated, topWatched }
2. Add ?year= to existing two endpoints
3. New endpoint, one list, ordered by query param (consolidates the existing two)

My lean: option 1. Trade-off documented in plan file.

Touch points once chosen:
- dbGetTopUserFilms (dataController.ts:677) — add optional year param, EXTRACT(YEAR FROM watched) filter
- New controller handler in statsController.ts
- Route wiring in statsRoutes.ts (two registrations for optional :year)
- Client API in src/client/services/api.ts:171

title: Implement working password reset (email link → /reset-password page → updateUser)
description:
Current state (cleaned up in PR #79): the 'Send reset email' half works (request hits Supabase, email goes out). The 'confirm new password' half was dead code with a vestigial token-paste UI that the server ignored. PR #79 removed the broken half. This issue is the proper implementation.

## What's missing today

- No /reset-password route in src/client/App.tsx
- The email link from Supabase's resetPasswordForEmail points to /reset-password but it 404s
- The user has no way to actually finish a reset after clicking the email link

## The right shape (Supabase pattern)

1. User clicks email link → lands on /reset-password with a recovery code in the URL hash
2. Page calls supabase.auth.exchangeCodeForSession(...) or relies on the JS SDK auto-handling the hash
3. With an authenticated session, the page calls supabase.auth.updateUser({ password })
4. Optional: redirect to /dashboard or /login on success

## Scope

- New ResetPasswordPage component
- New /reset-password route in App.tsx
- Reuses (or re-adds) AuthController.confirmPasswordReset OR does the updateUser call entirely client-side via the Supabase JS SDK using the session in hand
- Tests for the page (loading state, success, expired-token error, etc.)

## Out of scope

- Two-factor or other identity hardening
- Changing email addresses (different flow)

Source: PR #79 cleanup, 2026-05-20.

title: Introduce <Input /> primitive + cn() utility (component library foundation)
description:
First component in the new bpdiscord component library (Path B chosen: build our own, no shadcn). Used by no callers in this issue — atomic infrastructure.

Library principles (committed across future primitives):
1. Props extend native HTML attributes, never omit them
2. Accept ref as a regular prop (React 19 pattern). Type as Ref<HTMLElementType>
3. cn() for className; caller wins on conflicts via tailwind-merge
4. A11y via native attributes (aria-*, role) passed through
5. No implicit context for primitives. Composed components (Tabs, Tooltip, etc.) may use internal context to coordinate sibling pieces, but never expose context as a public API consumers must wrap.
6. Filename PascalCase matching the export, in src/client/components/ui/

## Scope

- Install runtime deps: clsx, tailwind-merge
- Create src/client/lib/utils.ts exporting cn(...inputs: ClassValue[]): string
- Create src/client/components/ui/Input.tsx:
  - interface InputProps extends InputHTMLAttributes<HTMLInputElement> with optional ref: Ref<HTMLInputElement>
  - function component, ref destructured from props (no forwardRef — React 19)
  - className composed via cn() — caller wins on conflicts
  - Inline the existing .input-field styles directly so tailwind-merge can resolve overrides (.input-field stays in index.css for legacy callers)
  - aria-invalid:border-red-500 aria-invalid:focus:ring-red-500 (Tailwind built-in modifier; verify Tailwind 3.2+ at install time, fall back to arbitrary variant if older)
- src/client/__tests__/Input.test.tsx — contract tests:

  - className override: pass 'px-2', assert px-4 is NOT in the class list (proves tailwind-merge is working)

title: Stage 1: app_users table + signup-time linking (FK to Users, auto-stub, auto-enqueue scrape)
description:
Foundation for linking Supabase Auth accounts to Letterboxd usernames. Lenient model — accept any well-formed lbusername at signup, admin fixes invalid ones later.

Schema (new supabase migration):
- CREATE TABLE app_users (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES auth.users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    lbusername VARCHAR UNIQUE REFERENCES "Users"(lbusername) ON DELETE SET NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
  );
- NO email/name columns — those live in auth.users.
- Backfill: INSERT INTO app_users(id) SELECT id FROM auth.users ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
- Drizzle documentary declaration in src/server/db/schema.ts.
- Verify migration applies cleanly on 'supabase db reset' before pushing.

authController.signup updates:
1. Normalize lbusername to lowercase. Validate format: 2-15 chars, [a-z0-9_-].
2. If lbusername provided: pre-check uniqueness in app_users (409 if claimed).
3. supabase.auth.signUp (creates the auth user).
4. Drizzle transaction:
   a. INSERT INTO "Users"(lbusername, is_discord=true) ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING.
   b. INSERT INTO app_users(id, lbusername).
5. If the Users row was newly created in 4a, enqueue a userScrapeJobs row so the LB data populates async.
6. If 4b fails with unique violation (concurrent claim race): compensating delete of the auth user; return 409.

Always insert an app_users row at signup, even when lbusername is NULL. Makes the admin list query trivial: SELECT * FROM app_users.

Error responses surfaced from signup:
- 400 'Letterboxd.com usernames must be 2-15 characters; letters, numbers, hyphens, underscores only.'
- 409 'This Letterboxd.com username has already been claimed.'

Out of scope: admin endpoints (Stage 2), signup form UI (Stage 3), admin page (Stage 4).

title: DecadeBreakdown widget — per-user decade table (profile page + reusable)
description:
## Goal
A self-contained widget that shows a single user's film library broken down by decade: how many they've watched, how many they've rated, and their average rating per decade. Initial home is the new profile page, but designed as a drop-in widget — anywhere in the app that has a username, you should be able to render `<DecadeBreakdown username="..." />` and have it work.

Supersedes bpdiscord-8zk (the two-user era-affinity comparison metric on the compare page). The single-user version is strictly more useful, easier to ship, and reusable.

## Display

```
🎞️ Decade Breakdown
─────────────────────────────────
Decade   Watched  Rated   Avg
1970s       5       3    4.0
1980s      12       8    3.8
1990s      32      20    3.5
2000s      45      30    3.6
2010s      60      50    3.7
2020s      20      15    3.6
```

- Only show decades the user has at least one film in. No zero-fills for empty decades.
- `avgRating` shown as `formatRating(avg)` when `rated > 0`, em-dash `—` otherwise.
- Tabular numerals (`tabular-nums`) so digits line up in columns.
- Sort ascending by decade.

## Server

New query function `dbGetDecadeBreakdown(username)` in `dataController.ts`:

```sql
SELECT
  (FLOOR(f.release_year / 10) * 10)::int AS decade,
  COUNT(*) AS watched,
  COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE uf.rating > 0) AS rated,
  AVG(uf.rating) FILTER (WHERE uf.rating > 0) AS avg_rating
FROM "UserFilms" uf
JOIN "Films" f ON uf.film_slug = f.film_slug
WHERE uf.lbusername = $1
  AND f.release_year IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY decade
ORDER BY decade;
```

- INNER JOIN — only counts films with a known `release_year` (~99.8% post-bw3 backfill).
- `FILTER (WHERE rating > 0)` distinguishes watched-but-unrated from rated. `rating = 0` is the project convention for "logged but not rated."
- `avg_rating` is NULL when `rated = 0` for that decade. Drizzle returns it as `null`.
- Server returns numeric decade (`1970`); client formats as `"1970s"`. Don't pre-format on the server — keep the data sortable/groupable.

## API endpoint

`GET /api/film-users/:username/decade-breakdown`

- Belongs under `/api/film-users` (public DB-only reads per CLAUDE.md), not `/api/users` (JWT-authed app accounts).
- Rate-limited consistent with sibling endpoints under `/api/film-users` (~120 req / 60s per IP — match what's there).
- 404 if the username has no UserFilms rows (consistent with other film-users behavior).

Response:
```ts
interface DecadeRow {
  decade: number;        // 1970
  watched: number;
  rated: number;
  avgRating: number | null;
}

interface DecadeBreakdownResponse {
  username: string;
  decades: DecadeRow[];
}
```

## Client architecture

Three pieces, each independently usable:

**1. Service method** (`src/client/services/api.ts`):
```ts
apiService.getDecadeBreakdown(username: string): Promise<DecadeBreakdownResponse>
```

**2. Hook** (`src/client/hooks/useDecadeBreakdown.ts`):
```ts
function useDecadeBreakdown(username: string): {
  data: DecadeRow[] | null;
  loading: boolean;
  error: string | null;
}
```
Encapsulates fetch + state. Matches the existing `useAwardShows` / `useHaterRankings` patterns.

**3. Widget component** (`src/client/components/DecadeBreakdown.tsx`):
```tsx
<DecadeBreakdown username="kevinyamada" />
```
- Self-fetches via the hook.
- Renders loading state (Spinner), error state, empty state, and the table.
- Takes only `username` as required prop. Optional `className?: string` for embedding context.
- No fixed margins — embed-friendly. Outer card styling (`className="card"`) is up to the caller if they want it.

## Out of scope (separate tickets if pursued)

- Two-user comparison view (was the original bpdiscord-8zk scope; defer indefinitely unless a real use case surfaces)
- Visualizations beyond the table — histogram, sparkline, "your decade" callout
- Genre / director breakdowns (similar pattern, separate data)
- Filtering / sorting controls

## Tests

- Server: query test against fixture data — known counts per decade, null avg when `rated = 0` for a decade, films with NULL `release_year` excluded.
- Hook: loading/success/error transitions (mock fetch).
- Component: smoke test for table render + empty state.

Reuse the existing testing patterns from `useHaterRankings.test.ts` and `HaterRankings.test.tsx`.

## Acceptance

- Endpoint returns the documented shape, rate-limited, handles unknown username with 404.
- Widget renders without parent context: `<DecadeBreakdown username="kevinyamada" />` works dropped into any page.
- Empty / loading / error states all render sensibly.
- Tabular-nums on numeric columns.
- No regression in existing comparison or stats pages.

## Where it lands first

The new profile page is the target, but it doesn't exist yet. Two options:
1. Build the widget standalone, embed temporarily in a dev/admin page or as a sibling on UserComparison to dogfood.
2. Build it in the same PR as a profile-page redesign (larger scope).

Recommend option 1 — ship the widget first, profile redesign is its own scope.

title: TasteCompatibility: decade affinity using Films.release_year
description:
## Goal
Compare two users by which film *eras* they prefer. Surfaces a dimension Pearson can't: two users can be 80% rating-aligned but love wildly different decades of cinema. Sharp insight that scores high on "memorability."

Unlocked by the recently-completed `Films.release_year` backfill (bpdiscord-bw3).

## What to compute

For each user:
1. Bucket their *rated* films by decade (1970s, 1980s, ..., 2020s).
2. Convert to a distribution (% of their ratings per decade).

Then:
- **Decade affinity** = cosine similarity of the two distributions. "How similar is the era profile of what you watch?"
- **Era polarization** = decade with the largest absolute difference. "User 1 = 60% post-2010, User 2 = 60% pre-1990."

## Display

Below the anchor films:

```
🎞️ Era affinity: 84% — you both lean 70s and 2010s
```

Or, when affinity is low:

```
🎞️ Era affinity: 31% — you watch different cinematic eras
   (User 1: 60% post-2010 · User 2: 60% pre-1990)
```

## Data requirement

The current `MovieInCommon` type carries `{title, user1_rating, user2_rating}` — no release_year. Two options:

1. **Backend change**: extend the `/api/comparison` (or wherever moviesInCommon comes from) to include `release_year` per film. Lightest change. Recommended.
2. **Client-side join**: client makes a second request to fetch year-per-slug. More client work, more requests. Not recommended.

Worth checking what the backend currently returns vs. what's needed — `Films.release_year` is in the DB; just needs to land in the API response.

## Edge cases

- Films with `release_year IS NULL` (the ~46 stragglers from bw3) → exclude from the distribution.
- Sample too small per decade → still compute the cosine, but flag when distributions are concentrated in 1-2 decades (low statistical confidence).
- One user has no films from a decade the other does → contributes asymmetrically; cosine handles this naturally.

## Out of scope

- Per-genre affinity (requires genre data not yet in Films)
- Director affinity (requires structured cast/crew, exists in ag_actors but only for sampled films)
- Time-of-watching analysis (when did they watch, not when was the film made)

## Acceptance

- Pure function `computeEraAffinity(user1Films, user2Films)` in `lib/ratingsCompatibility.ts`
- Returns `{ affinity: number, biggestGap: { decade: string, user1Pct: number, user2Pct: number } | null }`
- Unit tests for identical era preferences, opposite eras, partial overlap
- Backend changes filed as a sub-issue if API extension is required
- Rendered in TasteCompatibility card
close_reason: Superseded — pivoted from two-user era-affinity metric on the compare page to a single-user decade-breakdown widget on the profile page. Filed as a new issue with simpler scope.

title: TasteCompatibility: hot take count (films with ≥2★ rating gap)
description:
## Goal
Add a "hot takes" count to the TasteCompatibility card — films in the both-rated set where ratings differ by ≥ 2 stars. Concrete number tied to a specific human behavior (strongly disagreed), more engaging than abstract similarity.

## Spec

```ts
function countHotTakes(films: RatedFilm[]): number {
  return films.filter(
    (f) =>
      f.user1_rating > 0 &&
      f.user2_rating > 0 &&
      Math.abs(f.user1_rating - f.user2_rating) >= 2,
  ).length;
}
```

Reuse the same finite/positive guards as `computeCompatibility`.

## Display

Add to the sample-size line in TasteCompatibility:

```
47 films in common · 0.6★ apart on average · 8 hot takes
```

Or its own line if it crowds the existing summary.

## Future expansion (out of scope)

- Click "8 hot takes" → list view of the actual films
- Separate "love-it-hate-it" sub-count (one ≥4, other ≤2) vs "disagree" sub-count (gap ≥ 2 but not polarized)

## Acceptance

- `countHotTakes()` exported from `lib/ratingsCompatibility.ts` with unit tests
- Rendered in TasteCompatibility card secondary text
- Returns 0 (not null) for empty input

title: TasteCompatibility: anchor films — shared darling + biggest fight
description:
## Goal
Add two concrete-film callouts to the TasteCompatibility card. Numbers are abstract; specific titles are memorable and tell a story the metrics can't. Most engaging single addition to the card.

## What to show

- **Shared darling** — the film you're both most aligned on at the top of both scales. "You both gave _Mulholland Drive_ 5/5."
- **Biggest fight** — the film you disagree most on. "_Magnolia_: 1.5 vs 4.5 — one of you can't get enough."

## Selection logic

### Shared darling
Pick the film from the both-rated set that maximizes:
```
score = (user1 + user2) / 2 - |user1 - user2|
```
i.e. high average rating minus the gap. Penalty term breaks ties in favor of agreement.

Optional: gate on both ratings ≥ 3.5 so "darling" actually means "both like it" — without the gate, a pair that has zero films above 3 would surface a 2.5/2.5 "darling" that reads weird.

If no qualifying film, omit the callout.

### Biggest fight
Largest `|user1 - user2|` from the both-rated set. Minimum gap of 2★ — below that it's not a fight. Tiebreak by sum (a 5 vs 1 wins over 4 vs 2 — bigger total signal).

If no qualifying film, omit the callout.

## Display

Below the existing percentage + label + sample size block, before any low-sample warning. Two compact rows:

```
🌟 Shared darling: Mulholland Drive (5.0 / 5.0)
⚔️ Biggest fight: Magnolia (1.5 / 4.5)
```

Or possibly with poster thumbnails if data is available — check what `MovieInCommon` carries (currently `{title, user1_rating, user2_rating}`).

## Edge cases

- One or both films missing → show only the present one.
- Multiple ties → deterministic pick (alphabetical by title).
- Low sample (< MIN_RELIABLE_SAMPLE) → still show anchor films, they don't depend on statistical reliability.
- Pearson null (zero variance) → anchor films may still surface a fight if MAD is large; show them.

## Out of scope

- Click-through to film detail page
- Poster fetching if not already in `MovieInCommon` (would need API change)
- Multi-anchor lists (top 3 darlings, top 3 fights)

## Acceptance

- Pure functions for `findSharedDarling()` and `findBiggestFight()` in `lib/ratingsCompatibility.ts` returning `MovieInCommon | null`.
- Unit tests covering: clear winner, ties, no qualifying film, single-row input.
- Render in TasteCompatibility card.

title: moviemaestro: add release_year_attempts column so failed Films stop re-queueing
description:
## Goal
Add an attempt-tracking column to Films so the /backfill-film-years endpoint stops re-trying permanent failures every batch. Surfaced during bpdiscord-bw3 execution: 46 unique failing slugs (Letterboxd 404s) were each re-attempted ~4360 times because the SELECT `ORDER BY film_slug` deterministically picks the alphabetically-first NULL rows every batch, and failed rows stay NULL.

## Impact observed in bw3
- 200,583 failure records logged for 46 unique films
- Failures file bloated to 44MB
- Worker wasted hours of throughput re-trying known-bad slugs in the tail of the run
- Productive rate dropped from 91/batch to ~65/batch as the failure pool grew

## Schema change (bpdiscord side)
Migration adding a nullable smallint:
```sql
ALTER TABLE "public"."Films"
  ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS "release_year_attempts" smallint DEFAULT 0;
```

## moviemaestro endpoint changes
- SELECT: `WHERE release_year IS NULL AND release_year_attempts < :max_attempts` (default 3)
- On failure: `UPDATE "Films" SET release_year_attempts = release_year_attempts + 1 WHERE film_slug = $1`
- On success: leave `release_year_attempts` alone (the row drops out of the queue via `release_year IS NOT NULL`)

## Why now
Two reasons to file but not fix immediately:
1. The current backfill is done — 99.8% populated. The 46 stragglers can be triaged manually one-off.
2. Future top-up runs (after more Letterboxd users get scraped and more films are discovered with NULL `release_year`) would re-encounter this problem at smaller scale. The fix is worthwhile but not urgent.

## Acceptance
- Migration applied to prod
- moviemaestro endpoint uses the new column to filter + increment
- A test run that includes a deliberately-broken slug (e.g. one that 404s) sees `release_year_attempts` increment correctly and the row drops out of subsequent SELECTs after 3 attempts

title: Replace cosine similarity in TasteCompatibility with Pearson + MAD
description:
## Goal
The current TasteCompatibility card shows cosine similarity between two users' rating vectors. In practice it lands at 95–99% for almost every pair, which is misleading: that's a mathematical artifact of cosine on positive-valued vectors with similar means (most users rate in the 3–4★ band), not a true statement about taste alignment. Replace it with metrics that carry real signal.

## Scope

### Primary metric: Pearson correlation
Equivalent to cosine on **mean-centered** rating vectors. Captures whether two users deviate from their own average in the same direction — the actual question "do you like the same movies."

```
a_i' = a_i - mean(a_both_rated)
b_i' = b_i - mean(b_both_rated)
pearson = (a' · b') / (||a'|| × ||b'||)
```

Range: -1 to +1. Real values: ~0.6+ aligned, ~0.3–0.5 typical, ~0 no correlation, < 0 systematic disagreement.

### Secondary metric: Mean Absolute Difference (MAD)
`MAD = (1/n) × Σ |a_i - b_i|` over both-rated films. Displayed as "0.6★ apart on average." Intuitive, easy to grok, complements the abstract Pearson number.

### Drop the imputation logic
The current code imputes missing ratings with each user's average (with reduced confidence weights). This is incorrect for Pearson — imputed values collapse to "we both rate at our averages, perfectly correlated" and inflate the number. Use the **both-rated** set only. Display "N films in common" as the sample size; gate or de-emphasize display when N < 10 (single films dominate the metric below that).

## UI shape

- Headline number: Pearson as a signed percentage (e.g. "+47%" or "-12%"). Bar centered at 0, fills right/left based on sign and magnitude.
- Subtitle: MAD as "0.6★ apart on average · N films in common"
- Labels: "Strongly aligned" / "Aligned" / "Mixed" / "Diverging" / "Opposite"
- Warning when N < 10: "Sample size is small — interpret with caution"

## Acceptance

- Cosine similarity calculation removed; Pearson + MAD calculated on both-rated films only.
- Existing prop interface (`moviesInCommon`, `user1Data`, `user2Data`) unchanged.
- Edge cases handled: zero films in common, one user has zero variance (gave all the same rating).
- Pearson percentage and MAD both render correctly in the existing card layout.
- Typecheck clean (`yarn build`).

## Out of scope (separate follow-ups)

- "Hot take" count (films differing ≥ 2★)
- Anchor films ("your shared darling" / "your biggest fight")
- Decade affinity using the new `release_year` column
- Jaccard overlap metric

These belong to a richer multi-grain card pattern that's worth its own design pass once Pearson + MAD prove the swap is worth it.

title: Execute Films.release_year backfill (ag_films SQL shortcut + worker remainder)
description:
## Goal
Populate `Films.release_year` for existing rows. Stage 4b — the operational/execution half of the backfill, after the worker endpoint exists (bpdiscord-iz4).

## Three steps, in order

### 1. Measure

```sql
SELECT
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE release_year IS NULL) AS total_null,
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE release_year IS NULL AND tmdb_link IS NOT NULL) AS reachable_via_tmdb_shortcut,
  count(*) FILTER (WHERE release_year IS NULL AND tmdb_link IS NULL) AS scrape_only
FROM "Films";
```

`scrape_only` is the only number that determines worker pacing. If it's a few thousand, run overnight. If it's tens of thousands, plan a multi-day cron with failure alerting.

### 2. SQL shortcut from ag_films

Free, instant, partial coverage — any film with a TMDB link that's already in `ag_films` gets its year copied:

```sql
UPDATE "Films" f
SET release_year = a.release_year
FROM ag_films a
WHERE f.release_year IS NULL
  AND a.release_year IS NOT NULL
  AND (regexp_match(f.tmdb_link, '/movie/(\d+)'))[1]::int = a.tmdb_id;
```

- Uses `regexp_match` to extract the TMDB ID from URL shapes like `/movie/27205` *and* `/movie/27205-inception` (don't use a `LIKE` or anchored regex — the `-slug` suffix is common).
- Do **not** bump `updated_at`. Same reasoning as the worker endpoint.

Re-run the measurement query after this step to confirm coverage drop matches expectation.

### 3. Worker batch loop for the remainder

Drive the `POST /backfill-film-years` endpoint (bpdiscord-iz4) in a loop until `processed == 0`:

```bash
while true; do
  resp=$(curl -sS -X POST "$WORKER_URL/backfill-film-years" \
    -H "Authorization: Bearer $WORKER_SHARED_SECRET" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"batch_size": 100}')
  processed=$(jq '.processed' <<<"$resp")
  [ "$processed" -eq 0 ] && break
done
```

Tracker script can live in `src/server/scripts/backfillFilmYears.ts` if you want it checked in, or just be a shell one-liner — your call.

## Acceptance

- Pre-/post-counts show `total_null` drops to a small known set (films Letterboxd can't resolve — deleted pages, etc.).
- A sample of 20 randomly-picked backfilled rows have correct years (spot-check against Letterboxd).
- `updated_at` distribution on Films has not shifted en masse (confirms backfill didn't touch it).
- Remaining NULL rows are surfaced via a one-shot audit query so they can be triaged manually.

## Note
Stage 2 (bpdiscord-p9j) doesn't have to be deployed before this runs — the worker endpoint can populate years independently. But once Stage 2 is live, the per-user scrape path also organically backfills as users refresh.
close_reason: Backfill complete. 19621/19667 = 99.8% populated. 46 unique stragglers remain (Letterboxd 404s on deleted pages). Follow-up filed for iz4 attempt-tracking column.

title: moviemaestro: add POST /backfill-film-years endpoint
description:
## Goal
Add a backfill endpoint to moviemaestro that scrapes `release_year` for `Films` rows where it's NULL. Stage 4a of the four-stage rollout. The execution of the backfill is tracked separately (Stage 4b).

## Why moviemaestro and not a Node script

Per CLAUDE.md, all Letterboxd-touching code lives in moviemaestro — bpdiscord is a thin orchestrator. Keeping the scrape concentrated in one process preserves that invariant and means worker hardware (already sized for scraping) handles the load.

## Endpoint

```
POST /backfill-film-years
  body: { batch_size: 100, dry_run: false }
  auth: Bearer ${WORKER_SHARED_SECRET}
  response: { processed: int, updated: int, failures: [{ film_slug, error }] }
```

Behavior per call:

1. `SELECT film_slug FROM "Films" WHERE release_year IS NULL ORDER BY film_slug LIMIT :batch_size` (service-role key, bypasses RLS).
2. For each slug, fetch the Letterboxd film page via `letterboxdpy` and extract the year.
3. `UPDATE "Films" SET release_year = $1 WHERE film_slug = $2 AND release_year IS NULL`.
   - **Guard `AND release_year IS NULL`** so concurrent runs and Stage-2 upserts that land mid-batch don't race.
   - **Do not bump `updated_at`** — this is bookkeeping, not a content update. Consumers may use `updated_at` as a freshness signal and shouldn't see noise from backfill.
4. On parse/fetch failure: append to `failures`, leave row as NULL, continue.
5. Respect whatever per-page pacing the worker uses for normal scrapes — don't invent a new pace.

`dry_run=true` returns counts without writing.

## Acceptance

- Endpoint authenticates via `WORKER_SHARED_SECRET`.
- A batch of 10 known-good slugs updates the rows correctly.
- A batch including a row that's already non-NULL leaves that row untouched (guard works).
- `updated_at` unchanged on backfilled rows.
- Endpoint is idempotent: running the same batch twice produces no double-writes and `updated == 0` on the second call.

## Out of scope
- Actually executing the backfill across the whole table (Stage 4b, separate issue).
- Refactoring the existing scrape paths.

title: Sync Drizzle schema and Supabase types for Films.release_year
description:
## Goal
Add `release_year` to the Node-side type surface so downstream code can reference it without typecheck errors. Stage 3 of the four-stage rollout.

## Scope

1. **Drizzle schema** — `src/server/db/schema.ts`, in the `films` pgTable:
   ```ts
   releaseYear: integer('release_year'),
   ```
   Per CLAUDE.md this is documentary; the SQL is the source of truth, but keeping Drizzle in sync prevents type-inference drift.

2. **Supabase types** — regenerate `src/server/types/supabase.ts` **against prod** (not local) so the `Films` Row/Insert/Update blocks include the new field:
   ```bash
   supabase gen types typescript --project-id $SUPABASE_PROJECT_REF > src/server/types/supabase.ts
   ```
   Note: must run after Stage 1 (bpdiscord-09x) has been applied to prod. Running against a DB that doesn't have the column yet will silently emit a stale type.

## Acceptance

- `yarn typecheck` (or `tsc --noEmit` equivalent) passes.
- `films.releaseYear` is referenceable in Drizzle queries.
- `Database['public']['Tables']['Films']['Row']['release_year']` resolves to `number | null`.
- No controller / route / client code changes in this PR — pure type sync.

## Out of scope
- Any code that actually reads or writes `release_year` (future work, unblocked by this issue).

title: Trim CLAUDE.md — cut ~81% (1078→206 lines)
description: CLAUDE.md had grown to 50KB / 1078 lines. Most of it was derivable from the codebase (project tree, per-table columns, UX-flow prose, algorithm snippets, ASCII data-flow diagrams, full Vercel deployment section). Trim to non-obvious architecture, workflow rules, and gotchas Claude can't recover from the code or git history.

title: Isolate test DB from smoke DB (or properly reset between tests)
description:
Discovered while landing bpdiscord-8z2 (local smoke setup). The smoke seed (yarn setup:local creates admin@local.test in local Supabase) and the test suite both connect to the same local Supabase instance via DATABASE_URL_TEST. Many server tests assume a specific known DB state, so the smoke-seeded row makes them fail (e.g. "returns 409 when an lbusername is already claimed" passes the 409 check incorrectly because a row IS already in the DB).

Pre-existing fragility: test files don't call resetDatabase() in beforeEach. Setup.ts has it but the per-file usage is inconsistent. With prod-pointing .env, this was hidden (test DB hit local-but-mostly-empty Supabase). With smoke setup landed, the conflict is visible.

Options:
- A. Per-file beforeEach(resetDatabase) discipline. Simplest. But slow (full table truncates per test).
- B. Add a 'smoke' postgres schema in local Supabase, point smoke seed at it, leave 'public' for tests. Cleanest separation but requires migration restructuring.
- C. Use a second logical database in the local Supabase instance (e.g. postgres_test database). Postgres-level isolation. Need to bring up via supabase config.toml or psql -c 'CREATE DATABASE'.

Recommend A first (fast win), then consider B or C if tests start needing isolation guarantees.

Acceptance:
- 'yarn setup:local && yarn test' both pass cleanly
- Each test file resets the relevant tables in beforeEach
- Document the convention in CLAUDE.md (testing section)

title: Configure custom SMTP for production auth emails (Supabase)
description:
Currently using Supabase's shared SMTP, which is rate-limited to ~4 outbound auth emails per hour on the free tier. Fine for kicking the tires; not viable for real signups, password resets, or any meaningful user volume.

Surfaced during PR #79 QA when repeated signup testing exhausted the quota and produced 'email rate limit exceeded' errors.

## Scope

1. Pick a transactional email provider. Options worth evaluating:
   - **Resend** — Tailwind-ecosystem-friendly, generous free tier (100/day), simple SMTP + API
   - **Postmark** — strong deliverability rep, free 100/month, paid plans straightforward
   - **AWS SES** — cheapest at scale, more setup, requires DKIM/SPF config
   - **Mailgun** — established, decent free tier
2. Configure DNS records on your sending domain (SPF, DKIM, DMARC) — required for Gmail/Outlook to not flag the emails as spam
3. In Supabase Dashboard → Authentication → SMTP Settings → enable 'Custom SMTP' and paste the provider's credentials
4. Customize the auth email templates (signup confirmation, password reset, magic link, change-email confirm) — at minimum: sender name, subject, body wording, and the callback URL. Defaults work but read like Supabase boilerplate.
5. Re-enable 'Enable email confirmations' in Supabase Auth if it was turned off for dev (it should be on for prod)
6. End-to-end test: signup + password reset both deliver to a real inbox and the user can complete the flow

## Why this matters

- **Reliability**: rate limits go from ~4/hour to provider-tier (Resend free = 100/day, paid scales further)
- **Deliverability**: custom domain in From: address + proper DKIM/SPF = significantly better inbox placement vs. shared 'noreply@supabase.co'
- **Branding**: bpdiscord-from-bpdiscord-domain looks legitimate; supabase-from-supabase looks like phishing
- **Cost**: free tiers on Resend/Postmark cover the foreseeable volume; SES is essentially free at any reasonable volume

## Adjacent

Should land before any broader user onboarding push. Adjacent to bpdiscord-c5j (auth security posture audit) but distinct — that audit covers token storage / CSP / sanitization, this is purely infra config.

title: Audit: auth security posture — token storage, refresh tokens, CSP, sanitization
description:
Adjacent to but distinct from bpdiscord-znx (API response PII audit). Surfaced during bpdiscord-2on planning — the question 'is returning access_token in the response a risk?' opened the broader question of overall auth hardening.

## Background

The current auth flow:
1. /api/auth/login and /api/auth/signup return { data: { access_token, user } } in the response body
2. Client persists access_token + user to localStorage
3. Subsequent API calls send Authorization: Bearer <token>

This is the standard SPA token-in-localStorage pattern. It is NOT itself a vulnerability — it's the universally used contract. But it has well-known tradeoffs that are worth surveying deliberately.

## Scope of the audit

1. **Token storage**: localStorage vs httpOnly cookies. The latter is XSS-resistant (JS can't read HttpOnly cookies) but requires:
   - Server-side cookie management (wrap Supabase Auth)
   - CSRF protection (cookies bring CSRF risk that bearer tokens don't)
   - Touching every API call path
   Multi-day project minimum. Recommend deciding whether to attempt.

2. **Refresh token handling**: AuthResponse type only carries access_token. Supabase's session object includes a refresh_token that we currently discard. Default access token TTL is ~1 hour, so users get logged out hourly. Decide whether to wire refresh.

3. **CSP headers**: server uses helmet for some defaults. Audit the Content-Security-Policy directives — do we have one set explicitly, or is it the helmet default? A strict CSP is the most effective defense-in-depth against XSS (which is the actual route to localStorage exfiltration).

4. **Sanitization audit**: anywhere we render user-controlled HTML (dangerouslySetInnerHTML, raw markdown rendering, etc.)? Even one such surface is the XSS vector that makes the token-storage question urgent.

5. **Token logging**: LoginPage logs access_token.length (not the value). ✓ Spot-check other code paths for similar discipline. Look for any logger that might capture full request bodies (which would include passwords).

6. **user_metadata in localStorage**: also contains role='admin' for admins. If XSS exfiltrates, attacker knows who's admin. Marginal added exposure but worth noting.

7. **Token TTL configuration**: confirm Supabase's default 1h TTL is what we want. Shorter = more re-auth (annoying but more secure). Longer with refresh = better UX but enlarges the window of stolen-token validity.

## Deliverable

Written report grouping findings as:
- **Fix now** (file follow-up issues)
- **Worth deciding** (architectural tradeoffs — present options + recommendation)
- **OK as-is** (with one-line rationale)

This is research, not code changes.

title: Migrate .input-field callers to <Input /> + delete the CSS class
description:
Cleanup of the temporary duplication introduced by bpdiscord-x2l. The <Input /> primitive inlines the .input-field styles directly so tailwind-merge can resolve className overrides. .input-field stays in src/client/index.css for legacy callers; this issue migrates them.

## Scope
- Find every <input className='input-field' ...> in src/client (and any with input-field in a className expression)
- Replace each with <Input ... />
- Delete the .input-field rule from src/client/index.css
- Verify no remaining references to input-field anywhere

## Why
Two sources of truth for input styling is fine temporarily, but becomes drift the longer it sits. Easy mechanical cleanup once <Input /> ships.

title: Loading-state Button primitive + replace .btn-primary inline calls
description:
Second primitive in the component library. Same principles as <Input /> (bpdiscord-x2l).

## Scope
- src/client/components/ui/Button.tsx
  - extends ButtonHTMLAttributes<HTMLButtonElement>
  - ref as prop (React 19)
  - additional prop: loading?: boolean — when true: disabled + spinner (or other loading indicator)
  - className composed via cn(); caller wins on conflicts
  - inline the .btn-primary styles (similar pattern to Input)
- Migrate existing <button className='btn-primary'> callers to <Button>
- Tests: contract tests + loading-state behavior

## Why
Today every form button is bare <button> with manual disabled handling on submit. Double-clicks fire two requests. A primitive button with a baked-in loading state solves this once for every form.

## Open design questions to settle when picking this up
- spinner style (built-in vs. composition via children)
- whether loading auto-implies disabled (probably yes)
- how to handle aria-busy

More detail to be added before implementation.

title: Audit: API response bodies for PII / id / sensitive-info exposure
description:
Defensive-security audit. Motivation: PR #75 review (2026-05-18) caught a 409 conflict response on PUT /api/admin/users/:id that was leaking the claimer's id + email in a 'claimedBy' field. The endpoint was admin-gated so the info wasn't broadly exposed, but the pattern (PII in error bodies) ages badly if the validation is ever reused on a less-privileged surface.

This audit should walk through every endpoint and confirm responses don't leak more than callers should see.

## Scope

Walk every endpoint under src/server/routes/*.ts + corresponding controllers in src/server/controllers/*.ts. For each, inventory:

1. **Success response shape** — what fields are in 2xx responses?
2. **Error response shape** — what's in 4xx/5xx bodies, including the catch-all 500s?

Specifically look for these patterns:

- **Other users' emails or IDs in responses** when the caller isn't asking about them. The recently-fixed 'claimedBy' was the canonical example. Watch for any field that names a DIFFERENT user than the one being acted on.
- **Full Supabase auth user objects** returned to clients. authController.signup + login return signInData.user wholesale — that includes user_metadata.role, app_metadata, identities[], last_sign_in_at, etc. For self-data this is mostly fine but worth a deliberate check on what's actually being surfaced.
- **Raw error.message values from upstream services** that may include DB constraint names, SQL fragments, file paths, internal hostnames. Specifically: anywhere code does 'error: signUpError?.message' or similar passthroughs.
- **Stack traces** in error responses (should never happen, but check the global error handler in src/server/middleware/errorHandler.ts).
- **Internal IDs** (UUIDs, job ids, etc.) that aren't useful to the caller. Most of these are fine for the owning caller; flag any that show up in OTHER users' contexts.
- **Headers** that leak server info — X-Powered-By is normally suppressed by helmet but verify.

## Adjacent

- Related task bpdiscord-zom (error-handling consistency audit) already covers HTTP status codes and error response shapes. There will be overlap on the error-body inventory. If both audits land together, the recommendations should be coordinated.
- Logging is out of scope for THIS task (what console.error writes is a separate concern — logs go to a different threat surface) but worth a sibling audit later if motivated.

## Out of scope

- Public-by-design data (TMDB ids, Letterboxd lbusernames, follower counts scraped from public profiles). These are public on the source-of-truth sites; surfacing them in our API is fine.
- Self-data (a user reading their own profile, their own jobs, etc.). The audit is about leaking OTHER users' info OR system internals.

## Deliverable

A written report (markdown or bd notes) listing every endpoint with response shape inventory + flagged findings. Group findings:
- **Real leaks** (filed as separate bd issues with fixes)
- **Defensible but worth narrowing** (advisory)
- **OK as-is** (with one-line rationale)

Don't change code as part of this audit — file follow-up issues for each real finding.

title: Audit: error handling consistency across server controllers + middleware
description:
Audit how the Express server reports errors to clients and logs them internally. Suspicion: patterns drift across controllers — some return 2xx with an 'error' field in the body, some return 500 for known client errors, some leak stack traces, etc.

Inputs to audit:
- src/server/controllers/*.ts (auth, data, scraper, refresh, scrape-user, event, graph, etc.)
- src/server/middleware/errorHandler.ts (current global handler)
- src/server/routes/*.ts (per-route limiters + handler wiring)
- src/server/db/utils.ts (dbOperation, dbMutation, dbTransaction wrappers and their error→DbResult mapping)

What to check:
1. **Response shape consistency** — every error should follow {error: string, details?: unknown}. Look for the ad-hoc patterns: {message: '...'}, 2xx with error in body (signup pre-fix had this), bare strings.
2. **HTTP status codes**
   - 4xx for client errors (400 validation, 401 auth, 403 forbidden, 404 not found, 409 conflict, 422 unprocessable, 429 rate)
   - 5xx for server-side problems
   - Don't return 200/201 with an error field
   - Don't return 500 for known client-side error states
3. **Logging**
   - console.error vs structured logger (is there one?)
   - log message includes context (route, user id where safe, error code)
   - don't log secrets (tokens, full request bodies)
4. **Information disclosure**
   - error.message can sometimes contain DB constraint names or query strings — sanitize before sending to client
   - stack traces should never go in the response body
5. **Async error propagation**
   - 'async (req, res) => {...}' handlers without try/catch will surface as unhandled rejections (Express 5 catches these; Express 4 does not — verify which version)
   - Promise rejections inside callbacks need explicit handling
6. **Try/catch placement**
   - Per-controller catch-all 'catch (err) { 500 }' hides specific errors — favor narrow catches with classified responses
   - dbOperation/dbMutation/dbTransaction wrap DB errors into DbResult — caller controllers must still translate {success:false, error} into the right HTTP response
7. **Conventions worth codifying**
   - Single 'errors.ts' helper that maps known error types to HTTP responses?
   - Express error middleware vs in-handler responses?
   - Postgres error code map (23505→409, 23503→422, 22P02→400, etc.)?

Deliverable: a written report (markdown file or bd notes) listing every inconsistency found, with file:line refs and a recommended fix. Group recommendations into 'fix immediately', 'codify as convention', 'low-priority cleanup'. Then file follow-up issues for the immediate-fix items.

This is research/inventory work — don't change code as part of this audit. The follow-up issues do the cleanup.
notes:
Specific known findings to investigate during the audit (deferred here from the Stage 1 review on 2026-05-17):

1. authController.signup — signUpError path (lines ~71-78) returns 400 for ALL Supabase signup errors. Should differentiate:
   - 'User already registered' → 409
   - Rate limit → 429
   - Service unavailable → 503
   - Weak password / invalid email format → 400
   This is inconsistent with the signIn error handling in the same function (which now differentiates email_not_confirmed → 201 from other errors → 500). The signUp path should follow the same model.

2. authController.signup — optional-chaining inconsistency on signInData. After 'if (signInError) return', Supabase guarantees signInData.user and signInData.session are non-null. Line ~199 uses 'signInData.user?.email' (defensive ?.), line ~209 uses 'signInData.user!' (non-null assertion). Pick one.

3. authController.signup — '(err as { code?: string }).code' pattern appears at line ~128 (dbErr) and line ~170 (signInError). Same shape, two casts. A typed helper would clean it up:
     function errorCode(e: unknown): string | undefined {
       return typeof e === 'object' && e !== null && 'code' in e
         ? (e as { code?: unknown }).code as string | undefined
         : undefined;
     }
   This is a 'convention worth codifying' candidate per the audit scope.

title: Identity verification for Letterboxd.com username claims (follow-up)
description:
Known limitation of the lenient signup flow (per Stage 1, bpdiscord-cqg): a user can claim any Letterboxd.com username at face value. Once claimed, the rightful owner can't claim it (uniqueness blocks them), and the app shows the claimant 'their' data which actually belongs to someone else.

Current mitigations:
- Admin can reassign via /admin/users (Stage 4, bpdiscord-da2).
- 1-to-1 uniqueness enforced at the DB.
- Small trusted Discord community where admin oversight is practical.

Approaches to consider if abuse becomes a real problem:
- Verification token: user adds a one-time string to their Letterboxd bio; we re-scrape to confirm before linking.
- Confirmation flow: send the claim to admin for manual approval before activating.
- Risk-scoring: flag claims that conflict with other signals (e.g. scraped data shows a different Discord-linked identity).

No work needed now. This issue exists as a tracker so the limitation isn't forgotten.

title: Convert relative imports to path aliases
description:
Replace deep relative imports (e.g. "../../shared/utilities", "../shared/types") with TypeScript path aliases like @shared/*, @server/*, @client/*. Currently the repo uses relative paths everywhere, which gets noisy as the tree grows and makes file moves painful.

Scope of changes:
- Add "paths" entries to both src/server/tsconfig.json and src/client/tsconfig.json (and root tsconfig.json if it ends up affecting anything).
- Wire up matching aliases in src/client/vite.config.js (Vite resolve.alias) so the dev server + build resolve them.
- Wire up Vitest config so client tests still resolve aliases.
- Wire up the server build/runtime — note that Vercel's @vercel/node compiles TS but doesn't honor tsconfig paths at runtime without help (tsconfig-paths or a tsc post-process). Decide between (a) tsconfig-paths/register, (b) tsc-alias to rewrite emit, or (c) a bundler step. (c) is overkill for now.
- Codemod all existing imports. ts-morph or a careful find/replace per directory.

Acceptance:
- yarn tsc --noEmit passes in both src/server and src/client
- yarn dev boots client + server with no resolution errors
- yarn build succeeds and the deployed server can resolve aliased imports at runtime on Vercel
- All existing tests pass

Context: Surfaced 2026-05-14 while consolidating shared types into src/shared/types.ts. Deferred from that task because aliases touch Vite, Vitest, and the Vercel server build — bigger blast radius than the type consolidation warranted.

title: Delete /api/scraper/stream-films SSE route + exclusively-used Puppeteer helpers
description:
Step 5 of the bpdiscord-aiy /fetcher migration. The cutover landed in 7010d84 — /fetcher now uses the moviemaestro flow via useScrapeJob + JobProgress. The legacy SSE route is still mounted as a fallback during soak.

Pick up after /fetcher has been observed working in prod for at least a few days with real user traffic.

Scope:
1. Delete GET /api/scraper/stream-films/:username route from src/server/routes/scraperRoutes.ts
2. Delete scrapeUserFilmsWithProgress from src/server/scraperFunctions.ts (verified only used by the SSE handler — cronController and other paths use scrapeUserFilms without progress).
3. Delete the SSE-context helpers (initializeSSEContext, setupProductionTimeout, etc.) if they have no other callers.
4. Delete the fetchFilms handler from scraperController.ts.
5. DO NOT remove the Puppeteer deps from package.json — 6 other code paths still depend on them:
   - cronController.refreshAllUsers (nightly cron at 2 AM)
   - cronController.refreshUser (manual per-user cron)
   - /api/scraper/getUserRatings
   - /api/scraper/getUserProfile
   - /api/film-users/:username/ratings?fallback=scrape
   - /api/film-users/:username/complete?fallback=scrape

Acceptance:
- The SSE endpoint returns 404 (route deleted, not 500).
- Other Puppeteer-using endpoints still work (smoke test the cron path).
- yarn tsc --noEmit clean.
close_reason: Obsolete after bpdiscord-5rn (a163b95): the entire Puppeteer + SSE stack was deleted, including everything in this issue's scope plus the Puppeteer deps themselves (which this issue had explicitly excluded). Nothing left to delete.

title: Letterboxd rate-limit hardening: gap + circuit breaker + UI surface
description:
Follow-up to bpdiscord-aiy. After /fetcher migration ships, moviemaestro will have a single asyncio.Semaphore(1) wrapping all letterboxdpy entry points (see app/pipeline/letterboxd_throttle.py in moviemaestro). That gives serial execution and curl_cffi session safety but no gap enforcement, no circuit breaker, and no human-readable cooldown surface.

Pick this up when:
- We observe an AccessDeniedError or HTTP 429 in moviemaestro production logs, OR
- A user reports their refreshes are failing globally, OR
- We decide to raise MAX_CONCURRENCY above 1 (any concurrency change should land with these protections)

Design (researched and pre-decided in the bpdiscord-aiy planning thread):

1. Minimum gap between requests — leaky-bucket, ~750ms with ±100ms jitter to match the existing safe Puppeteer pacing in cronController/scraperFunctions (constants.ts:14 FILM_SCRAPING_DELAY=750).
2. Circuit breaker: open on letterboxdpy.core.exceptions.AccessDeniedError or InvalidResponseError(code=429). Cool down for 15 min (CIRCUIT_OPEN_SECONDS=900). Configurable.
3. Phase loops bail early when circuit_open_seconds_remaining() > 0 — don't grind through 100 more failing items, append_log a clear abort line and break.
4. CircuitOpenError exposes seconds_remaining so the orchestrator can surface a human-readable error in errors[]: 'letterboxd cooldown active, Nm remaining' — visible in the bpdiscord UI as the job's failure reason.
5. Tests: semaphore caps concurrency, gap enforced, circuit opens on both AccessDeniedError and 429, auto-closes after cooldown, jitter bounded. Plus an integration test with 5 fake concurrent orchestrator tasks asserting serial execution with ≥ MIN_GAP - jitter between calls.

Out of scope but worth noting in a future bd:
- Session rotation if cookies get tainted (letterboxdpy's Scraper._session is a process-wide singleton).
- TLS fingerprint variation (letterboxdpy is locked to curl_cffi's impersonate='chrome' — no rotation).
- Railway egress IP rotation / residential proxy if IP-banned.

Context: Letterboxd has no published rate limits. Their API explicitly refuses 'data-analysis projects' per support.letterboxd.com/hc/en-us/articles/15269070369551. We operate on tolerance, not permission. Existing cron (cronController.ts:92) paces 2sec/user via Puppeteer and has been unbanned for months. The per-user /fetcher flow is unlikely to trigger bans at our user volume (<10 concurrent, peak ~4 req/s) which is why this hardening is deferred until observed signal.

title: Skip known-bad film slugs in refresh pipeline
description:
Some film slugs in UserFilms reference URLs that 404 on Letterboxd (e.g. blank-check-on-broadway, the-actor-awards-2026-presented-by-sag-aftra). They were valid when scraped from a user's diary at some point but the films have since been renamed or removed on Letterboxd. Every refresh re-attempts them in phase 3 (film_ratings) and they fail with ResourceNotFoundError.

This is harmless (per-item failures don't abort the run; they accumulate in errors[]) but it bloats the errors panel and wastes one HTTP request per bad slug per refresh.

Implementation options to evaluate at pickup time:

1. Auto-tombstone on 404: when scrape_and_upsert_film catches ResourceNotFoundError, write a row to Films with film_slug=slug, lb_rating=NULL, and a marker like url='UNREACHABLE'. The get_missing_films() RPC stops returning it because the row exists.
   - Pro: zero manual intervention, self-healing
   - Con: if the film comes back (slug was renamed back), it stays excluded; would need a periodic 'retry stale tombstones' job

2. Add a 'skipped' boolean column to Films. Worker upserts skipped=true on ResourceNotFoundError. RPC filters WHERE NOT skipped. Admin UI exposes a 'forget skip' button per row to retry.
   - Pro: explicit, observable, manual override is easy
   - Con: schema change + admin UI work

3. Separate skipped_films table keyed on film_slug. Same idea as #2, kept out of the main Films table.

Recommend #2 if we want admin control, #1 if we just want quiet operation.

Acceptance:
- After one refresh, films that 404 don't appear in errors[] on the next refresh
- Some mechanism to retry skipped films when desired
close_reason: Fixed in moviemaestro 0ca0c49 via auto-tombstone (option #1 from the issue). When phase-3 scrape raises ResourceNotFoundError, orchestrator writes a Films stub row (url='unreachable', lb_rating=NULL) so get_missing_films() stops returning the slug. Manual retry: DELETE the Films row. Surfaces a 'tombstoned' counter on progress and per-tombstone log_tail lines for observability. Auto-deploys to Railway from main.

title: Migrate /fetcher per-user scrape from Puppeteer to moviemaestro
description:
The /fetcher page (https://www.thebigpicdiscord.com/fetcher) currently uses POST /api/scraper/stream-films/:username on the bpdiscord backend, which spins up Puppeteer to scrape one user's films and streams progress via SSE. This works but is slow, memory-heavy, and risks hitting Vercel function timeouts on large libraries.

moviemaestro already does this scrape using letterboxdpy + curl_cffi (no browser, ~10x faster, vastly less memory). Once bpdiscord-bix proves the Railway deployment in production for the bulk refresh job, we should reuse that infrastructure for the per-user case too.

Scope of work (rough — re-plan when picked up):

1. moviemaestro side
   - Add a single-user entry point. Two reasonable shapes:
     a. Repurpose orchestrator with a 'scope' parameter on refresh_jobs (full | one-user)
     b. New endpoint POST /scrape-user (Bearer auth) that takes username, runs scrape_and_upsert_user_films, writes a job row
   - Decide: does single-user need its own table, or share refresh_jobs with a 'scope' column?

2. bpdiscord side
   - New admin endpoint that triggers the single-user scrape (likely POST /api/admin/refresh-user/:username)
   - Reuse the polling pattern from useRefreshJob (rename to useScrapeJob or generalize)
   - Update /fetcher page to use the new flow instead of EventSource against /api/scraper/stream-films
   - Decide the auth model: should /fetcher be admin-only (matching new admin endpoints) or stay open to any authenticated user?

3. Cleanup (optional, after migration is live)
   - Retire src/server/scraperFunctions.ts scrapeUserFilms* (Puppeteer code path)
   - Remove @sparticuz/chromium and puppeteer deps if nothing else uses them

Open design questions to resolve at pickup time:
- Refresh_jobs schema reuse vs separate table (probably reuse, but progress shape would differ)
- Whether to consolidate the bulk + per-user endpoints under one admin route family
- Whether to keep the SSE flow as a fallback during rollout

Acceptance:
- /fetcher page uses moviemaestro for scraping (no more Puppeteer in this path)
- Per-user scrape latency drops measurably vs current
- Existing UX preserved (user enters username, sees progress, sees completion)
close_reason: Migration complete. /fetcher now triggers POST /api/scrape-user/:username which hands off to moviemaestro's parameterized orchestrator (table='user_scrape_jobs', lbusername=<name>). Same 3-phase pipeline as bulk refresh but scoped to one user, with per-username SQL single-flight allowing concurrent scrapes for different users. UserRatings histogram refreshed in phase 1 (bonus: bulk path gets this too). letterboxdpy serialized through asyncio.Semaphore(1) for cookie-jar safety. Shipped across 8 commits: moviemaestro 9b15934 / a116b6f / 4df9ac4 / e585522; bpdiscord 40b0b5e (migration, approved + applied) / c876249 / b4c09e6 / 2c03e35 / 7010d84. Deferred follow-ups: bpdiscord-yao (rate-limit hardening on first observed 403), bpdiscord-0qz (delete SSE route + exclusively-used Puppeteer helpers after soak).

title: moviemaestro JobState terminal writes should guard WHERE status='running'
description:
moviemaestro app/pipeline/job_state.py's complete() and fail() write {status: ...} unconditionally. If admin cancels in the small window between the worker's last is_cancelled() poll and its terminal write, the worker overwrites status='cancelled' with 'completed' or 'failed'.

Window: ≤ one per-item iteration latency (typically 5-10s for film_ratings phase, less elsewhere).

Fix: add a WHERE-clause guard at the SQL level so terminal writes only land on still-running rows:

  supabase.table('refresh_jobs').update({...}).eq('id', job_id).eq('status', 'running').execute()

mark_cancelled() doesn't need this guard (it intentionally finalizes an externally-flipped cancel state).

Found during code review of bpdiscord-smc (admin endpoints PR). Not a blocker for shipping that PR — the API-side cancel already does its job; this only affects the visible terminal state if the race fires.
close_reason: Fixed in moviemaestro 069b074: complete() and fail() now use .eq('status', 'running') guard so an admin cancel that races with the terminal write isn't clobbered. Added two regression tests in tests/test_job_state.py covering the race for both terminal paths. Auto-deploys to Railway from main.

title: Disambiguate Independent vs Opposite in TasteCompatibility tooltip
description:
## Problem
The current tooltip describes Independent as "you watch the same films but on different wavelengths." That phrase actually fits Opposite better (predictable inverse — out-of-phase) and doesn't capture what Independent (Pearson ≈ 0) really means: *no* relationship, no predictability either way.

## Fix
Rewrite the three cases to lead with the predictability framing:

- Aligned: tend to react the same way
- Opposite: tend to react in opposite directions (predictably)
- Independent: no pattern at all — sometimes agree, sometimes don't, but you can't predict it

One-line copy change in `TasteCompatibility.tsx`. No logic touched.

Open follow-ups: bpdiscord-16v (terminal-write race), bpdiscord-aiy (migrate /fetcher off Puppeteer), bpdiscord-vpk (skip known-bad slugs).

Env file layout for bpdiscord (saved 2026-05-14, after cleanup).

CANONICAL STRUCTURE — only TWO env files:

1) src/server/.env  — server-side secrets, loaded at runtime
   - Loaded by dotenv.config() with no args in src/server/server.ts
   - Server runs via 'cd src/server && yarn dev', so cwd=src/server, dotenv reads ./.env
   - Contains: SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_ANON_KEY, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, JWT_SECRET, DATABASE_URL, DATABASE_URL_TEST, PORT, ENABLE_SCRAPER, TMDB_API_KEY, TMDB_READ_API_TOKEN, WORKER_URL, WORKER_SHARED_SECRET, CRON_SECRET, PUPPETEER_SKIP_CHROMIUM_DOWNLOAD

2) src/client/.env  — Vite-exposed public vars only
   - Loaded by Vite (no custom envDir), cwd=src/client
   - Only VITE_* prefixed vars are exposed to the browser
   - Contains: VITE_HOT_RELOAD (and VITE_API_URL if overriding the proxy default of /api)

3) ROOT /.env  — DELETED 2026-05-14, do not recreate
   - Was an orphan (likely from running 'vercel env pull' at project root)
   - Server's dotenv does NOT walk up the tree, so root .env was never loaded at runtime
   - Most insidious bug it caused: setting PUPPETEER_SKIP_CHROMIUM_DOWNLOAD=true here would silently break local scraping if any shell hook (direnv, etc.) loaded it before 'yarn install'
   - Setting WORKER_URL/WORKER_SHARED_SECRET here looked like it should work but didn't — server reads src/server/.env ONLY

PUPPETEER_SKIP_CHROMIUM_DOWNLOAD nuance:
   - Local dev: leave EMPTY in src/server/.env so 'yarn install' downloads Chromium and Puppeteer scraping works
   - Production: set to 'true' via vercel.json's env block (not via .env), since Vercel uses Sparticuz chromium binary

If you ever run 'vercel env pull', cd into src/server first so it writes to the right .env (or specify --env-file=src/server/.env). Pulling from root will recreate the orphan.

Local Supabase stack for bpdiscord — workflow recovery (saved 2026-05-14).

PREREQS:
- Docker Desktop must be running (whale icon solid in menu bar). If 'supabase status' returns 'Cannot connect to the Docker daemon at unix:///Users/kevinyamada/.docker/run/docker.sock', Docker Desktop is not up yet.
- Run all supabase CLI commands from the project root (/Users/kevinyamada/projects/bpdiscord). The CLI uses ./supabase/config.toml to identify the project.

BRING UP:
- 'supabase start' — boots 13 containers named supabase_<svc>_bpdiscord (db, kong, auth, rest, studio, storage, edge_runtime, realtime, inbucket, pg_meta, analytics, vector + one more). ~30s when images are cached, ~2min cold.
- 'supabase status' — prints DB URL, anon key, service_role key, Studio URL. Postgres always at 127.0.0.1:54322.

VERIFY:
- src/server/.env has DATABASE_URL_TEST=postgresql://postgres:postgres@127.0.0.1:54322/postgres — that is what src/server/__tests__/setup.ts reads. If tests fail with ECONNREFUSED 127.0.0.1:54322, the stack isn't up.
- 'psql postgresql://postgres:postgres@127.0.0.1:54322/postgres -c "\dt"' lists tables.

MIGRATIONS:
- 'supabase db reset' — drops local DB and replays all supabase/migrations/* from scratch (use after pulling new migrations or if schema drifts).
- 'supabase migration new <name>' — scaffold a new migration file.

SHUT DOWN:
- 'supabase stop' — stops containers, keeps volume data so next start is fast.
- 'supabase stop --no-backup' — also wipes local data.

GOTCHAS:
- Don't be fooled by Docker Desktop's UI search filter — typing 'bpdiscord' shows partial results; the stack actually creates 13 containers all suffixed _bpdiscord.
- Orphan 'flamboyant_edison' storage-api container can cause port conflicts; 'docker rm flamboyant_edison' if so.
- The server unit tests in __tests__/dbUtils.test.ts and __tests__/utilities.test.ts do NOT need the DB. Everything else does.
